metadata.visit_title: Primary refractory transformed high-grade BCL with new CNS symptoms, treatment decision

condition_labels: Transformed follicular lymphoma / high-grade aggressive B-cell lymphoma (primary refractory)

code.text: Transformed follicular lymphoma with MYC amplification and IGH/BCL2 translocation, high-grade aggressive B-cell lymphoma, primary refractory to R-CHOP

Observation.valueString: New subacute back pain, bilateral lower-extremity weakness, urinary hesitancy

code.text: R-CHOP x6 cycles (first line); primary refractory

DiagnosticReport.conclusion: Unresolved, more intensely FDG-avid mesenteric mass; refractory disease.
Ordered urgently to evaluate suspected CNS involvement.

transcript:
DR: Mr. Okafor, good to meet you. I've been through your records. Before we get into the lymphoma, you mentioned some new symptoms to the nurse. Walk me through those.

PT: Yeah. About a week now. My lower back's been aching, but the thing that scared me is my legs feel weak, especially the right one. And I'm having trouble, sorry, trouble starting to urinate.

FAMILY: I'm Yvonne, his wife. He almost went down on the stairs Tuesday. That's not him.

DR: I'm glad you came in. Any numbness, any loss of control of your bladder or bowels?

PT: No accidents. Just the weakness and the trouble going.

DR: Okay. That combination, new back pain, leg weakness, and urinary hesitancy, in someone with aggressive lymphoma, that gets my full attention today. It can mean the lymphoma has reached the lining around the spinal cord and brain. I want an urgent MRI of the whole spine and brain, and I want to sample the spinal fluid.

PT: I thought this visit was about whether the chemo worked.

DR: It was going to be. Your first chemo, the R-CHOP, didn't clear the disease, the scan at the end showed the mesenteric mass was actually more active. So we already knew we needed a second plan. But what you just told me changes the shape of that plan before we even start.

FAMILY: Changes it how?

DR: If the lymphoma is in the central nervous system, the fluid around the brain and spine, then most of our usual second-line drugs don't get in there well enough. We'd have to pick treatments that cross into that space and also put chemotherapy directly into the spinal fluid. It reroutes everything. That's why I don't want to lock in a plan until I see the MRI and the spinal fluid.

PT: And if it is there?

DR: Then we treat hard and fast, because CNS involvement is serious, but it is treatable, and people do get through it. I'd move you to a stronger salvage chemotherapy that penetrates the nervous system, add chemotherapy given directly into the spinal fluid through repeated spinal taps, and aim to get the disease under control enough to go to CAR T-cell therapy, which is our best shot at a lasting remission for a lymphoma like yours.

PT: I want that shot. Whatever it takes. I'm 62, I coach my grandson's team, I'm not done.

DR: That's exactly the frame I want. And your fitness matters here, it's the reason I can offer you the aggressive path. If you were frail, I'd be having a very different conversation. You're not.

FAMILY: What does CAR-T involve? I've heard the word.

DR: We collect his own immune cells, re-engineer them to attack the lymphoma, and give them back. It's powerful, and it can cause a reaction, fevers, sometimes confusion, so it means time in or very near the hospital with close monitoring, and it means a caregiver at his side. That's where you come in.

FAMILY: I'll be there. Whatever the schedule is.

DR: That support is a real part of why this path is open to him. It doesn't work without it.

PT: So what happens first?

DR: First, the imaging and the spinal tap today or tomorrow, urgently. If the CNS is involved, we start the spinal fluid chemotherapy right away and begin the stronger salvage regimen. In parallel, I take your case to our tumor board this week so the transplant and CAR-T team is lined up. If there's disease left after salvage, we can use a bridging treatment to hold things steady while the CAR-T cells are being made. And we keep a stem-cell transplant from a donor in reserve, only if we ever need it after CAR-T.

PT: That's a lot.

DR: It is. We do it in order, one step at a time, and you will not be doing it alone. The reason I'm laying out the whole staircase is so you can see there's a destination, remission, not just a series of hard weeks.

PT: Okay. Let's find out what we're dealing with and go.

DR: That's the plan. I'll put in the MRI and the spinal tap now, start the protective medicines, and get you on the board this week. Yvonne, the same rule for you both: any new confusion, fever, worse weakness, or if he can't urinate at all, that's an emergency, come in.

FAMILY: Understood.

DR: We'll move quickly. You came in at the right time.
note:
**Assessment:** 62-year-old fit man with transformed follicular lymphoma (MYC amplification, IGH/BCL2 translocation), high-grade aggressive B-cell lymphoma, primary refractory to first-line R-CHOP. Presents with new subacute back pain, lower-extremity weakness, and urinary hesitancy concerning for secondary CNS involvement. ECOG 1, working full-time, strong caregiver support.

**Plan / reasoning:**
- Urgent MRI total spine and brain plus lumbar puncture with CSF flow cytometry to confirm suspected leptomeningeal and intrathecal disease. (Anticipated: leptomeningeal and intrathecal enhancement from the mid-thoracic level downward and posterior-fossa involvement, CSF positive for clonal B-cells.)
- If CNS confirmed: CNS-penetrating salvage (R-DHAP) plus intensive intrathecal methotrexate/rituximab; systemic control with intent to proceed to CD19 CAR T-cell therapy.
- Bridging therapy for residual disease pending CAR-T manufacturing; allogeneic transplant reserved only for post-CAR-T relapse.
- Present at tumor board this week to align the cellular-therapy team; aggressive path justified by fitness, goals, and caregiver support.
- Supportive/CNS prophylaxis and monitoring; safety-netting for cord-compression and neurologic emergencies.
after_visit_summary: Your first lymphoma treatment did not fully clear the disease, and the new symptoms you described today, back pain, leg weakness, and trouble urinating, raise concern that the lymphoma may have spread to the fluid around your brain and spine. We are getting an urgent MRI and a spinal tap to find out. If it has spread there, we will treat quickly with a stronger chemotherapy that reaches the nervous system, plus chemotherapy given directly into the spinal fluid, with the goal of getting you to CAR T-cell therapy, which offers the best chance at a lasting remission. We will discuss your case at our team conference this week. Because you are healthy and active and have strong support at home, we can offer you this aggressive, curative-intent path. Call us or come in immediately for any new confusion, fever, worsening weakness, or inability to urinate.

metadata.visit_title: New-patient evaluation, primary refractory DLBCL, treatment decision

code.text: Primary refractory diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, GCB subtype (BCL2+ / BCL6- / MYC- by IHC), stage III, spleen-dominant
History of ER-positive breast cancer, treated

medicationCodeableConcept.text: dexamethasone 20 mg daily x3 days after each cycle-1 epcoritamab dose (new)

code.text: Age-attenuated mini-R-CHOP x6 cycles (first line), completed 2026-03

DiagnosticReport.conclusion: Marked interval increase in splenic and mesenteric FDG activity; consistent with refractory disease.
Necrotic tissue; flow cytometry with no monoclonal B-cell population (equivocal).
DLBCL, GCB subtype; IHC BCL2 positive, BCL6 negative, MYC negative.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mrs. Ferreira. Thanks for making the trip in. I know it's a bit of a drive.

PT: About an hour and a half, but my daughter drove, so it wasn't bad. I'd rather come to the right place.

FAMILY: I'm Carla, her daughter. I come to the important ones.

DR: I'm glad you're both here. Before we talk about what's next, tell me how you're actually doing day to day.

PT: Honestly, pretty good. I still do my garden every morning, the tomatoes are coming in. I cook for myself, I drive to church and the store. The only thing is this ache on my left side, under the ribs, it's been nagging.

DR: That ache is almost certainly the spleen, which is where most of your lymphoma is. You're gardening every day?

PT: Every day it's not raining. I don't sit still well.

FAMILY: She really doesn't. She was up a ladder cleaning gutters last month and I about had a heart attack.

DR: Understood, no ladders while you're my patient. But that tells me a lot. On paper I see an 86-year-old, and a lot of people would look at that number and reach for the gentlest thing on the shelf. What you're describing is not that person.

PT: I don't feel 86. I feel like I have things left to do.

DR: That matters to me, and it changes what I recommend. Let me tell you where things stand. You had the R-CHOP, a lighter version because of your age, and it worked at first. But the recent scan shows the lymphoma is growing again, in the spleen and some lymph nodes. When it comes back this quickly, while we're basically still finishing the first treatment, we call that refractory. It's the tougher category.

PT: The biopsy people said they didn't find cancer in the spleen, though.

DR: They found dead tissue and no live cells they could type, which happens when a needle hits a necrotic spot. But the scan lit up brighter and bigger than before. I'm treating what the whole picture shows, not one needle. I'm confident this is active.

PT: Okay. So what do we do.

DR: Let me walk through the options out loud, because there's real judgment here. There's a newer pill-and-antibody combination that's easy to take and gentle. But it's specifically not the right tool when the disease is refractory the way yours is; it's meant for a different situation, and I don't want to give you something that looks convenient and buys nothing.

PT: So not that one.

DR: Not that one. Then there's the most aggressive thing we have, CAR T-cell therapy. In the right person it can be a home run. But it means living near the hospital for weeks, a lot of monitoring, and it's hard on the body in a way I don't think fits at 86, and you're an hour and a half away. I don't think that's your path, and I want to be honest about why.

PT: I don't want to move to New Haven for a month. My garden would die.

DR: That's a real consideration, not a small one. So that leaves two serious regimens. One is a gentler antibody pairing. The other is a stronger combination, a bispecific antibody called epcoritamab together with a chemotherapy pair called GemOx. The stronger one asks more of you, but given how well you're doing and what you've told me you want, I don't want to sell you short with the gentle one.

PT: I want the one that gives me the best shot. I didn't drive all this way to go easy.

DR: That's what I was hoping you'd say, and it's the right instinct for someone in your shape. Here's the honest part: the strong combination isn't the by-the-book choice for someone your age, and a computer following the rulebook would flag it. I'm choosing it because of who you are in front of me, not the number on your chart. I want you to understand that tradeoff and agree to it.

PT: I understand. I trust you. What does it involve?

DR: The bispecific has to be started carefully, with small stepped-up doses the first cycle, because it can cause a reaction. Those first doses need to happen here, where we're set up for it. After that, once you're through the ramp-up safely, I can hand the ongoing treatment back to Dr. Petrakis near your home so you're not driving here every week.

FAMILY: That's the part I was worried about. So most of it can be close to home?

DR: Most of it, yes. The intense early part here, the routine part there. I'll coordinate directly with his office.

PT: And how will I feel?

DR: The first cycle is the most watchful, we look out for fevers and that reaction I mentioned. You'll be on a few protective medicines, an antiviral and an antibiotic to prevent infections, and something for nausea. Most people your age who are as active as you get through the ramp-up well. If your body tells us it's too much, we adjust or step down. You're always in the driver's seat.

PT: Then let's do it. The strong one.

DR: We'll do it together. I'll get the baseline bloodwork and the hepatitis testing today, set up the first stepped doses here, and call Dr. Petrakis so he's ready to take the handoff. We'll rescan after about three cycles to make sure it's working.

FAMILY: What do we watch for at home?

DR: Fever over 100.4, shaking chills, confusion, or trouble breathing, any of those, you call and you come in, day or night. Don't wait it out.

PT: I'm not a waiter. Ask my daughter.

FAMILY: She is not.

DR: Good. That instinct will serve you well here. Let's get started.
note:
**Assessment:** 86-year-old woman with primary refractory DLBCL (GCB, BCL2+/BCL6-/MYC- by IHC), spleen-dominant stage III, progressing on imaging about four months after age-attenuated mini-R-CHOP. Repeat biopsy equivocal (necrosis, no clonal population), but serial PET and clinical picture are consistent with refractory disease. ECOG 1 with robust functional status (independent, physically active).

**Plan / reasoning:**
- Not a CAR T-cell therapy candidate given age and geographic distance from the cellular-therapy center; the logistics and expected toxicity outweigh benefit here.
- Tafasitamab-lenalidomide is not appropriate in primary-refractory disease and was excluded.
- Options discussed with the patient and the referring oncologist: polatuzumab + mosunetuzumab (lower intensity) versus epcoritamab + GemOx (higher intensity). Given her robust performance status and explicit goals, chose **epcoritamab + GemOx** to avoid under-treatment. This choice is acknowledged to diverge from strict guideline defaults for her age; rationale and patient agreement documented.
- Bispecific step-up dosing to be delivered at this center (cycle 1), with transfer of ongoing therapy to the local oncologist for proximity.
- Supportive care: acyclovir and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis, antiemetics, dexamethasone per step-up protocol.
- Baseline CBC, CMP, LDH, hepatitis B/C and HIV serologies today. Restaging PET/CT after 3 cycles. Close coordination with the referring oncologist.
after_visit_summary: Your lymphoma has come back and is active in your spleen and some lymph nodes, even though a recent biopsy was not clear, the scans and the overall picture show it is growing. Because you are doing so well and want to treat it strongly, we chose a stronger combination treatment: an antibody called epcoritamab together with a chemotherapy pair called GemOx. The first cycle will be given here so we can watch you closely as the antibody dose is slowly increased, then your ongoing treatment can move closer to home with Dr. Petrakis. You will take an antiviral and an antibiotic to prevent infection and medicine for nausea. Call us right away for a fever over 100.4, chills, confusion, or trouble breathing. We will repeat a scan after about three cycles to see how the treatment is working.
